original_abstract:
This review surveys the significant body of research that has emerged over the last decade following the increasing availability of large-scale, anonymized mobile phone datasets. Originally used as a proxy for network theory models, these call data records (CDRs) have evolved into a vital resource for "computational social science," offering non-self-reported insights into human behavior across millions of individuals.

The paper categorizes the existing literature into several key domains. First, it examines the topological structure of social networks derived from calls, highlighting how communication patterns differ from other complex networks through features like high clustering, heavy-tailed degree distributions, and the "strength of weak ties." Second, it explores the interplay between geography and communication, detailing how distance influences social link formation and how aggregated data can be used to partition countries into functional "social regions" or estimate regional socioeconomic status.

The survey further investigates the temporal dynamics of these networks, focusing on the "bursty" nature of human interactions and the stability of social ties over time. A major section is dedicated to mobility, reviewing how mobile traces reveal the high predictability of individual movements and the synchronized routines of urban populations. The review also covers the dynamics of information diffusion and the potential spread of mobile viruses. Finally, it addresses the practical applications of these findings—ranging from urban planning and traffic management to tracking infectious diseases and emergency response—while providing a critical overview of the technical and ethical challenges surrounding data de-identification and user privacy.
humanized_abstract:
Over the last decade, a plethora of studies have been published based on large anonymised mobile phone datasets that have become increasingly available. Such datasets, based on call data records (CDRs), were originally used as a proxy to validate network theory models. Today, however, they have evolved into a powerful tool for computational social science, providing hundreds of thousands to millions of individuals’ daily movements and interactions free of self-report bias.

The work classifies state-of-the-art literature into several main domains. We begin by analyzing the topology of social networks inferred from call data, and then discuss their differences with other complex networks through measurements such as clustering coefficient, heavy-tailed degree distribution, and “strength of weak ties”. We also explore how geography interacts with or underpins communication, and discuss the impact of distance on social link creation and also how to build aggregate country-wide graphs to identify social regions or to estimate the socioeconomic status of individual regions.

This survey is a completely theoretical paper, aiming to give a review on recent social network analysis works based on mobile phone data (also known as MHD works). This part briefly discusses the properties of social networks discovered by MHD works, such as node degree distribution, transitivity, community structure, burstiness and stability. More interestingly, this part discusses the dynamics of these networks, such as the characteristics of human communication behaviour due to the “bursty” nature and the stability of social relationships over time. This part concentrates on the analysis of the mobility pattern and behaviour which are extracted from mobile traces. It discusses in great detail the high predictability of individuals mobility pattern and the synchronized pattern of human activities in the city due to mobile phone data, which opens a window for city design and traffic optimization. The diffusion of information through mobile phones and the spread of mobile viruses are also discussed in this part. The last part concerns the applications of MHD works and some limitations. Based on the unique properties of MHD works which include high spatial and temporal resolution and unprecedented population coverage, this part lists many potential applications of MHD works, such as designing smart cities, optimising the traffic system, monitoring the spreading of infectious disease, improving the emergency system and so on. Finally, it discusses the technical and privacy challenges that such works may encounter, in particular concerning the privacy preserving data anonymisation issue.
